content_of_post_to_comment:
For years, I tried to brute force my way into good habits. I would rely on random bursts of motivation, go hard for three days, and then completely burn out. I always ended up back at square one, feeling guilty and lazy.

The problem was that I thought discipline was about punishing myself. I did not realize that I was actually dealing with deeply ingrained psychological self-sabotage.

I recently read a fascinating psychology book by Alden Rothman. Even though it uses finance and wealth as the main examples, the core message is entirely about human behavior and why we subconsciously ruin our own progress. It broke down exactly how my mental roadblocks were triggering my bad habits.

Once I understood the root cause of my behavior, I stopped fighting my own brain. Building discipline became a lot easier when it was not a constant internal battle.

Has anyone else here realized that their lack of discipline was actually just a deeper psychological issue, rather than just being "lazy"?
